ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for wheatgrass drink; FOODON_03301710 (plant-based beverage) used as closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (herbal and plant-based beverages) is the closest defensible category for a sweetened wheatgrass drink; no exact wheatgrass-specific code exists in the public FoodEx2 matrix.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for wheatgrass drink as a food product; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

allergens: wheat (wheatgrass; gluten content negligible in juice form but cross-contact risk exists)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve commercial wheatgrass drink in Singapore is sold in a 250 ml bottle or cup, equivalent to approximately 250 g.

nutrient_source.title: Wheatgrass drink — Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for Wheatgrass drink (cr_id D05010042). All nine reported nutrients are used as-is with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — sugar-sweetened beverages analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for commercial wheatgrass drink. GI estimated at 63 (medium) based on the GI of sugar-sweetened fruit-flavoured beverages and sucrose-based drinks (GI ~60–65) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. The drink's carbohydrate is almost entirely sucrose/sugar (8.4g/100g total carbohydrate = 8.4g sugar), which drives the GI estimate. GL per 250g serving = (63 × 21.0g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 13.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage — 8.4g sugar/100g with zero dietary fibre; SSB consumption independently raises blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Despite very low sodium (3 mg/100 g), the high sugar content (8.4 g/100 g) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage; the AHA (2018) links regular SSB consumption to elevated blood pressure, so individuals with hypertension should avoid this drink and choose unsweetened wheatgrass shots instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage — 8.4g sugar/100g, zero dietary fibre, medium GI (~63); rapid glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: This sweetened wheatgrass drink delivers 21 g of rapidly absorbed sugar per 250 ml serving with no fibre to blunt the glycaemic response, making it unsuitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes; unsweetened wheatgrass shots (pure juice, no added sugar) are the preferred alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular SSB consumption linked to elevated triglycerides, visceral adiposity, and CVD risk; fat and sodium are negligible
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While fat (0 g/100 g) and sodium (3 mg/100 g) are negligible, the 8.4 g sugar/100 g SSB classification is associated with elevated triglycerides and increased CVD risk with habitual consumption; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid sweetened wheatgrass drinks.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory sugar load (8.4g/100g) with zero fibre; no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients present in commercial sweetened form
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The added sugar in this commercial wheatgrass drink promotes inflammatory mediators and negates any potential anti-inflammatory benefit from wheatgrass chlorophyll; RA patients seeking wheatgrass benefits should choose freshly pressed, unsweetened wheatgrass juice.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wheatgrass drink is a commercially prepared, sweetened green beverage sold widely in Singapore and other parts of Southeast Asia, classified under Asian and herbal drinks. It is made from wheatgrass juice (extracted from the young shoots of Triticum aestivum) diluted with water and sweetened with sugar, giving it a light, mildly grassy flavour with a sweet finish. The drink is light green in colour and has a thin, watery texture. Per 100 g, it provides 34 kcal, 0.1 g protein, 0 g fat, 8.4 g carbohydrate (entirely as sugar), 0 g dietary fibre, and 3 mg sodium. The high sugar content (8.4 g/100 g) with zero fibre classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), which has clinical implications for Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease management. Despite the health halo associated with wheatgrass, the commercial sweetened drink form provides no meaningful anti-inflammatory or glycaemic benefit. The estimated glycaemic index is approximately 63 (medium), yielding a glycaemic load of about 13 per 250 ml serving. Individuals seeking the purported benefits of wheatgrass should opt for freshly pressed, unsweetened wheatgrass shots. The drink is vegan, vegetarian, dairy-free, and low in sodium, but is not suitable for diabetics or those managing blood sugar or cardiovascular risk due to its added sugar content.
common_user_queries: Is wheatgrass drink good for diabetes?
Is wheatgrass drink suitable for hypertension?
Wheatgrass juice vs wheatgrass drink difference

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D05010042) with confidence 0.95; exact match to the named food item. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sugar-sweetened beverages, clearly documented.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source — this is a water-based sweetened beverage with no added fat by formulation (confirmed zero). saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero by the same rationale. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo — commercial wheatgrass drinks are filtered/diluted and contain no measurable fibre (confirmed zero). iron_mg is reported as 0.0 per HPB Foodo (confirmed zero / below detection threshold). monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this commercial wheatgrass drink are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific product as of 2026. GI value of 63 is an analogous estimate based on sugar-sweetened beverages from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for commercial wheatgrass drink was found in approved sources. Allergen note: wheatgrass juice is derived from wheat shoots; while the gluten protein is primarily in the grain endosperm and not the grass, cross-contact risk exists and individuals with wheat allergy should exercise caution.
